name: Tomato

disambiguationNotes: Do not substitute canned tomatoes, sauce, paste, puree, or cooked tomato values.
Lycopene claims must stay tied to raw tomato unless a different form is intentionally sourced.
If a source row refers to tomato products rather than plain raw tomato, this file is the wrong target.

secondarySources.note: Exact row identified in this pass. This is an SR Legacy record with explicit nutrient-row anchors, though many tomato nutrient values are aggregated rather than single analytical rows.

changeNotes: Production draft upgraded with explicit USDA anchor fdcId 170457 / ndbNumber 11529.
Canonical row now attached; remaining blocker is that several useful tomato nutrient rows are aggregated or calculated rather than simple direct analytical rows.

kcal.sourceName: USDA FoodData Central API food details
kcal.note: Canonical anchor row for energy. Current file value 18 matches the attached row. Derivation is calculated (NC/source 4).

protein_g.sourceName: USDA FoodData Central API food details
protein_g.note: Canonical anchor row for protein. Current file value 0.9 is aligned with attached row value 0.88. Derivation is aggregated (JO/source 11).

fibre_g.sourceName: USDA FoodData Central API food details
fibre_g.note: Canonical anchor row for fibre. Current file value 1.2 matches the attached row. Derivation is aggregated (JA/source 6).

glycemic_index.note: Low GI is plausible for raw tomato, but the exact figure still needs an attached source outside USDA.

bioavailability_percent.note: Current protein bioavailability figure is only placeholder scaffolding and should not be treated as a production metric.

pros.title: lycopene is the clearest standout tomato compound
pros.explanation: Tomato has a genuinely food-specific lycopene story, which gives it more identity than just being a low-calorie watery vegetable.

evidence.sourceName: tomato / lycopene literature; exact citation still to be attached
evidence.citationNote: Supported concept, but the exact production citation bundle is still missing.

pros.title: very low energy density with everyday usability
pros.explanation: This is one of tomato's clean practical strengths and is directly consistent with the macro profile.

evidence.citationNote: Directly supported by the nutrient profile assuming the canonical row is later locked.

pros.title: easy low-downside everyday food
pros.explanation: Reasonable narration context, but not a claim that raw tomato is nutritionally elite.

cons.title: fibre is modest compared with stronger vegetables
cons.explanation: Tomato contributes some fibre, but it is not a standout on that axis once peers like kale enter the comparison.

evidence.citationNote: Directly supported by the nutrient profile.

cons.title: micronutrient density is decent rather than elite
cons.explanation: Tomato has useful upside, but the broader micronutrient panel is not dominant compared with top vegetables.

evidence.citationNote: Fair direction from the current metrics, though still draft until the canonical row is locked.

cons.title: acidity can be annoying for some people
cons.explanation: Useful real-world caveat, but too individual to present as a strong scoring claim.

sourceNotes: Draft production-lane raw-tomato entry now carries explicit USDA anchor fdcId 170457 / ndbNumber 11529.
Do not reuse this file for sauce, paste, canned, or cooked tomato forms.
Canonical row is attached, but lycopene support still needs a separate citation bundle and several metric derivations are aggregated rather than clean direct analyses.
